chapter: Chapter 3: Electricity: Circuits and their Components

questions.question: Define electric current.
questions.answer: Electric current is the rate of flow of electric charge through a conductor.

questions.question: State the SI unit of electric current.
questions.answer: The SI unit of electric current is ampere (A).

questions.question: What happens to current when resistance increases?
questions.answer: When resistance increases, the current in the circuit decreases.

questions.question: Why must an ammeter be connected in series?
questions.answer: An ammeter is connected in series so that the entire current passes through it for accurate measurement.

questions.question: Why is a voltmeter connected in parallel?
questions.answer: A voltmeter is connected in parallel to measure the potential difference across a component.

questions.question: Name the law that relates voltage, current, and resistance.
questions.answer: Ohm’s Law relates voltage, current, and resistance.

questions.question: What does the formula V = IR represent?
questions.answer: It represents Ohm’s Law showing the relationship between voltage, current, and resistance.

questions.question: Why do conductors allow current to flow easily?
questions.answer: Conductors have free electrons that move easily and carry electric charge.

questions.question: Why do insulators not conduct electricity easily?
questions.answer: Insulators do not have free electrons to carry electric charge.

questions.question: What is the main function of a fuse in a circuit?
questions.answer: A fuse protects the circuit by melting and breaking the circuit during excessive current.

questions.question: Why does a filament glow in an electric bulb?
questions.answer: The filament glows due to the heating effect of electric current.

questions.question: Which metal is commonly used in electrical wiring?
questions.answer: Copper is commonly used in electrical wiring.

questions.question: What is the function of insulation around wires?
questions.answer: Insulation prevents electric shocks and leakage of current.

questions.question: What happens when a circuit becomes open?
questions.answer: When a circuit becomes open, current stops flowing.

questions.question: What is the purpose of circuit diagrams?
questions.answer: Circuit diagrams represent electrical circuits using standard symbols.

questions.question: Why are thick wires used in high-current circuits?
questions.answer: Thick wires have lower resistance and can carry larger currents safely.

questions.question: What causes a short circuit?
questions.answer: A short circuit occurs when current flows through a path with very low resistance.

questions.question: What safety device automatically switches off a circuit during overload?
questions.answer: A circuit breaker automatically switches off the circuit during overload.

questions.question: What is the direction of electron flow in a circuit?
questions.answer: Electrons flow from the negative terminal to the positive terminal of a cell.

questions.question: Why should electrical appliances not be used with wet hands?
questions.answer: Water conducts electricity and increases the risk of electric shock.

questions.question: What type of circuit is commonly used in household wiring?
questions.answer: Parallel circuits are used in household wiring.

questions.question: What does resistance depend on?
questions.answer: Resistance depends on the length, thickness, and material of the conductor.

questions.question: Why are electrical devices connected in parallel at home?
questions.answer: They are connected in parallel so each device receives the same voltage and works independently.

questions.question: What is the purpose of earthing?
questions.answer: Earthing provides a safe path for excess current to flow into the ground.

questions.question: What happens when the resistance of a circuit becomes very high?
questions.answer: When resistance becomes very high, the current flowing in the circuit becomes very small.
